title: Te Whakamarumaru o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku / Guardians of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Science
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply connected to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe and gives our college its name. Students will explore the pūrākau of Hinepūkohurangi, the mist maiden whose tears formed our awa, while investigating the current health of the stream through scientific methods. This connects our Ngāti Kahungunu identity with modern environmental science.
learningObjectives: Measure and analyze water quality parameters (pH, turbidity, temperature) from three locations along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Compare traditional Māori indicators of stream health (presence of tuna, kōura, native vegetation) with scientific measurements
Design and propose one evidence-based intervention to improve the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Present findings using both scientific terminology and te reo Māori environmental vocabulary

activities.title: Whakapapa o Te Awa - Story of Our Stream
activities.description: Students listen to local kaumātua share the pūrākau of Hinepūkohurangi and how she formed Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Using large paper, students create visual timelines showing both the geological formation and the cultural story of our awa.

activities.title: Matauranga Māori Meets Water Testing
activities.description: At three sites along the stream (upstream, college boundary, downstream), students work in pairs to conduct water quality testing using scientific equipment while simultaneously recording traditional indicators - presence of tuna (eels), kōura (crayfish), and healthy native riparian plants.

activities.title: Data Korowai - Weaving Knowledge Together
activities.description: Using large collaborative maps of the stream, students plot their scientific data and cultural observations using different colored threads/strings, creating a physical 'korowai' (cloak) of knowledge that visually represents the health of different stream sections.

activities.title: Rongoā Planting - Hands-on Kaitiakitanga
activities.description: Students identify areas of stream bank erosion and plant native rongoā (medicinal) plants such as harakeke and kawakawa to stabilize banks, learning both the ecological benefits and traditional uses of these plants from local experts.

resources: Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu to share pūrākau
Water testing kits (pH strips, thermometers, turbidity tubes)
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' guide
Te Awa Tupua - Whanganui River legislation documents for comparative study

tasks: Create a detailed stream health report comparing scientific data and traditional indicators
Design a culturally-informed restoration plan for one section of the stream
Present findings to peers using both scientific terminology and te reo Māori

nzcAlignment: Nature of Science: Investigating in science, Understanding about science
Planet Earth and Beyond: Earth systems, Interacting systems
NZC Key Competencies: Participating and contributing, Using language, symbols and texts
